data.title: YPF: The Most Tangible Opportunity For Argentine Equity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The historic Argentine company is at an inflection point, consolidating its position as the country's leading integrated oil company. It is delivering strong results in operational efficiency and profitability, underpinned by a long-term strategy focused on shale development and export growth. Its importance in the development of Vaca Muerta is key, not only for the possibility of energy self-sufficiency, but also for the export potential it represents for the country.

The execution of the "4x4 Plan", the construction of the VMOS pipeline, and the preliminary agreements to export LNG are important points that position the company as the central axis of the new national energy agenda.

Although the macroeconomic environment remains complex and legal risks still generate uncertainty, I consider that YPF (NYSE:YPF) has revealed the ability to adapt: it improved margins, reduced relative leverage and managed to fund itself at competitive interest rates. Analyzing the valuation, the stock operates at strong discounts to the global sector, offering an attractive entry point for investors with a medium to long-term horizon. At the same time, its consolidation in operational and financial terms strengthens its flexibility in the face of external shocks and local volatility.

For all these reasons, I consider the risk-return ratio favorable, and I therefore recommend buying. YPF's operational dynamism, projected growth in production and exports, and the opportunity for significant appreciation if the regulatory framework normalizes make it a significant opportunity within the emerging asset market.

Overview

YPF S.A. is Argentina's biggest integrated energy company, which operates in hydrocarbons exploration, production, refining, and commercialization. The company maintains its leadership in the local oil and gas market and also in refining activities. Its role in the country's energy agenda not only strengthens the local markets but also export opportunities.

The macroeconomic conditions in which YPF operates persist as challenging. Argentina is implementing a structural adjustment program under the initiatives of the Milei administration. These adjustments include tax reforms, the gradual removal of energy subsidies, tariff realignment with the currency, and liberalized free-market pricing systems. In an attempt to restore the economic equilibrium and encourage investment, these measures, on the other hand, create short-term pressures: consumption is low, inflation is high, and changes in policy are frequent. This severely affects the energy industry due to its controlled prices, limited access to foreign currencies, and production incentives.

From this perspective, the most essential and with the greatest prospect of the company is located in Vaca Muerta, which is among the largest reserves of shale oil and gas in the world. YPF leads the development of this formation, representing nearly 40% of the country's unconventional production.

The company's current strategic plan aims to multiply its value by four in the next 4 years ("4x4 Plan"), supported by intensive investments in upstream and key infrastructure projects to enable exports. The construction of the Vaca Muerta Sur pipeline (VMOS), with an estimated capacity of 390,000 barrels per day (KBOE/D), and the agreements for future export of LNG to Asia, position YPF at the center of the national energy strategy. According to data from the government and the company itself, in 2025 alone it is expected to invest more than $3 billion in the development of unconventional gas.

Throughout 2025, YPF has consolidated signs of strategic progress that reinforce this expansion. In January, it successfully placed $1.1 billion in bonds at a rate of 8.5%, intended to repurchase short-term debt and improve its financial profile. A few weeks later, it signed a memorandum with Indian companies for the future export of LNG, in line with its objective of positioning itself as a regional energy supplier.

Also, the formation of a conglomerate of seven oil companies to advance with the VMOS pipeline reinforces its purpose to turn Argentina into an export hub. But complex fronts moreover persist, such as the 2012 expropriation litigation, which continues its course in US courts and represents an important potential liability for the State and for the company's balance sheet.

Altogether, YPF is operating in 2025 with a determined strategy, supporting in the development of unconventional resources and the internationalization of its energy business, as stated by its CEO, Horacio Marín, on April 11th in Freedom Hall on the 7th floor of the NYSE, under the phrase: "Delivering the full potential of Vaca Muerta Argentina". However, it is still exposed to the macroeconomic, judicial, and political risks that characterize the Argentine market.

4Q 2024 and FY 2024

YPF finished FY 2024 with improvements in its results, both in operating and financial terms, indicating that it is in a period of expansion linked to production and efficiency in unconventional developments.

Net revenues were $19.3 billion in FY 2024 (+11% YoY), explained by the rise in local fuel prices and growth in oil exports. In Q4 2024, net revenues fell 10%, to $4.75 billion, due to lower seasonal gas sales and the reduction of domestic fuel prices.

Adjusted EBITDA was $4.6 billion in FY 2024 (+15% YoY), and EBITDA margin was 24.2% in 2024 compared to 23.4% in 2023, and in Q4 2024 it reached $839 million (-39% YoY) and the margin was 17.6%.

Net income was positive: $2.4 billion in 2024, compared to a loss of $1.3 billion in 2023, and in Q4 2024, it had a loss of $284 million, contrasted to a profit of $1.48 billion in Q4 2023.

CAPEX reached $5.04 billion (-5% YoY), in line with YPF's target ($5 billion): 63.5% of this corresponded to non-conventionals investments, while FCF for 2024 was negative, at $760 million, 3% higher than 2023 and net debt ended FY 2024 at $7.43 billion, (+9% YoY), but the net debt / EBITDA ratio decreased to 1.6x compared to 1.7x in 2023.

At an operational level, YPF achieved a total production of 536,000 barrels equivalent per day (KBOE/D), representing a 4% YoY rise.

This increase was mainly explained by Vaca Muerta, where shale oil production increased by 21% and shale gas by 11%, leading to unconventional developments now representing 53% of the total produced by the company.

Valuation

The company has a low valuation relative to its energy sector peers, despite solid improvements in its fundamentals. The P/E (FWD) is 5.11x, a 46.5% discount to the average of the 17 largest companies in the sector (9.56x) and 44.2% compared to the top seven companies in the integrated oil and gas sector (9.16x).

Meanwhile, the EV/EBITDA (FWD) is located at 3.66x, 26.1% below the sector average (4.95x) and 10.9% below the top 7 (4.11x). Furthermore, the P/E ratio is 0.80x: although it is still below the sector (1.21x) and larger companies (1.1x), it is double its historical average of 0.48x, which allows me to observe an equity revaluation in line with improved return expectations.

These compressed multiples contrast with solid operating performance. EBITDA grew 261% YoY and EBIT 913.5%, with additional growth projections of 16.7% and 58.7%, respectively. The net margin (11.6%) already exceeds the sector average (9.1%), ROE has soared to 21.6%, more than double that of the sector (10.6%), and ROE (FWD) stands at 16.46%, well above expectations for the seven largest companies in the sector, supported by improved operating leverage and efficient use of capital. Operating cash generation grew 215% YoY, and the Price/Cash Flow (FWD) of 2.57x implies a strong discount with respect to the sector (4.52x).

However, challenges persist: FCF margin remains negative (-6.05%) due to an aggressive CAPEX policy (30.1% of sales), well above the sector (17.3%), aimed at expanding production, mainly in Vaca Muerta.

Added to this are risks such as potential tariffs or external restrictions, along with local political and judicial uncertainty, which partly explain the valuation penalty. Even in this scenario, I believe that the strong growth in revenue (+226% YoY), ROA (6.9%), and tangible book value (+66% YoY) supports a thesis of sustained operational recovery.

In the integrated analysis of YPF's indicators, I must highlight that it combines low multiples with improving fundamentals, strong operating growth, and expanding returns, suggesting high potential for revaluation if the macro and regulatory context stabilizes.

Risks

YPF faces a complex environment in which multiple sources of risk converge and influence its future performance. At the local level, the political transition in Argentina, with a government promoting accelerated economic liberalization, generates short-term uncertainty. While a more investment-friendly framework is being sought, the process entails a profound reconfiguration of subsidies, tariffs, and controls that could generate disruptions in the energy chain, social tensions, and regulatory volatility. In this context, any unexpected adjustment in internal fuel prices or changes in the sector's tax structure could affect demand, operating costs, or refining margins.

Additionally, Argentina's macroeconomic fragility must be taken into account: the scarcity of Central Bank reserves, the distortion in relative prices, and the need to implement fiscal corrections generate pressure on purchasing power, make imported inputs more expensive, and make financial planning difficult. In addition, the exchange gap and restrictions on currency transfers continue to be an obstacle for YPF's international operations, which must import equipment, pay foreign debt or distribute dividends.

Internationally, the energy landscape is increasingly volatile. The conflict in the Middle East, tensions in the Red Sea, and expectations regarding Chinese demand are generating uncertainty about crude oil prices in the coming months. Any fall in the Brent price would affect YPF's export revenues and deteriorate incentives to expand production at Vaca Muerta. Added to this are global financial conditions, with higher interest rates and greater risk aversion, which complicate access to capital in international markets, which could both delay project execution and increase the cost of debt.

Finally, the legal battle in New York remains a latent risk. Although the ruling is not yet final, the equity impact resulting from YPF's nationalization could influence investment decisions, market access, and the perception of country-company risk in the immediate future.

In short, the company is facing a period of great potential, but also one of high sensitivity to political, judicial, financial, and geopolitical factors that could define its performance in the coming months.

Conclusion

YPF is the key player in Argentine energy, not only for its performance but also for its privileged access to Vaca Muerta, one of the most important unconventional deposits in the world. As I analyzed, the company improved its results in recent years, showing increases in EBITDA and cash generation and profitability, combined with a reduction in financial risk.

In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at significant discounts to the industry average, both in terms of earnings multiples, cash flow and enterprise value. From my point of view, this mismatch between price and fundamentals is very important considering the growth potential associated with productive expansion and infrastructure projects that will allow crude oil and gas exports to increase in the coming years.

Although the macroeconomic context is still challenging, I consider that the repositioning of YPF in financial and operational terms, added to a regulatory environment that is beginning to normalize, shows a high probability of progressive valuation. For long-term investors, I recommend buying, as I believe the balance between risk and return is favorable.

data.title: Nvidia: A Generational Investment With Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
My Nvidia History

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is my second-best investment of all time, where at its peak, it returned a 200% return in less than 2 years. My original investment was sparked by a combination of the company's repetitive early mover investments in GPUs, data centers, and the launch of CUDA, the most ubiquitous AI coding platform, giving Nvidia a huge moat. In late 2023, I looked at Nvidia's financials, their history of earnings, and valuation at the time. I was shocked it was trading at only ~26x earnings. It was also apparent AI was going to be "the next big thing" and Nvidia as the player. The next day, I put about 30-40% of my portfolio into buying shares & shorting long-dated puts. It paid off rather quickly. The investment was based on relative valuation, the same method I use to judge investments in both the most boring companies as well as the flashiest.

Current Environment

Nvidia is among the few generational companies that are directly tied to an industrial disruption. Nvidia doesn't just sell chips, it sells the entire computing stack. It owns the architecture, controls the software, and supplies the hardware that every major cloud provider and enterprise relies on. This level of vertical integration is rare and already led to pricing power and high margins. The business model has shifted over the years from a cyclical semiconductor company, tied to gaming demand. This shift explains why the company's revenue mix changed drastically over the last five years and why its valuation has expanded. Gross margin reached a record 75% in Q4 FY25 (ending January 2025). This marks a significant jump from the 65% range last year and the company's historical average of 60%. I expect the 75% level to be maintained in the foreseeable future as the companies new Blackwell line ships out in volume. Looking at the bottom line, profit margin climbed significantly from ~20% to 55%.

Recently, at the GTC 2025, Jensen Huang reinforced Nvidia's competitive positioning as the backbone of the AI economy. Huang touched on several interesting topics, including the concept of "AI Factories", advancements in physical AI/robotics, and the Launch of Dynamo AI OS. The industry is growing fast and Nvidia is keeping up speed. Most tangibly, he reiterated that the launch of the Blackwell architecture is pushing the envelope in terms of performance and efficiency. Production began in Q1 '25, and they're designed for large-scale AI workloads. Early revenue has started, but the full ramp up is expected in the second half of 2025 when volume shipments go out. The market's response is overwhelmingly positive so far with major customers lining up, including Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META) and other cloud service providers.

Nvidia is not a company whose valuation climbed as the result of a bubble. It has a long history of profitability, and is realizing results today which make the company worth owning. For as exciting of a business Nvidia is, I invest based on valuation which is supporting attractive entry points today.

Historical Performance

I always start my analysis process with a review of historical performance, typically the last 5 years. As we are into 2025 or FY26, with the fiscal year ending in January '26, I'm using FY21 as the base period. See below:

If 2025 plays out as planned on a per-share basis, sales per share will have increased over 12x, cash flow and earnings will both increase over 21x+, and book value 9x. This represents huge annualized earnings growth between 55-91% a year. What I find most noteworthy here, is that Nvidia's share price is trailing its true growth at only a 17x increase, a fraction of its cash flow and earnings. This is one of many flags a company is undervalued.

Future Performance

Given Nvidia's size today, it will not keep up the same annualized rates of growth, but they'll trend on the higher end relative to other companies. I expect they'll continue to grow all fundamentals between 10-12% on an annualized basis out to FY31. I think it's easy to get carried away on future projections, and I prefer to base my investments on lower-end assumptions.

My FY26 EPS estimates are quite conservative. I'm projecting EPS of $4.37 vs. $4.51, Wall Street's average. Also, considering Nvidia typically beats Wall Street expectations, they are likely to beat my earnings estimate. Looking out to 2030, there aren't as many calls for earnings yet, but Wall Street estimates FY27 earnings at $6.41, and I'm using just $7.13 for my FY31 projections. This gives me an extremely high level of confidence that the price targets I set using my low-end estimates will hit. I use these growth rates in my final valuation, and I never want to overpay for a stock because I assumed it would magically grow indefinitely. If the investment case is still bright after all my adjustments, it's an easy decision for me to move forward.

Valuation

Nvidia is a high-growth company that will likely trade above its 10-year historical valuation in the coming years. I usually look at the average 5-10 year PE multiple and assume a reversion to those levels, but I'll incorporate a different method to determine an appropriate multiple for Nvidia. For reference, Nvidia traded at about 29x earnings the last 10 years and 36x the last 5. Clearly, multiple expansion is in play.

Determining An Appropriate Valuation Multiple

Considering reversions to the mean, and projected growth rates around 10% annualized, I see Nvidia's P/E falling somewhere between the 10-year average of 31x and the blended average of 44x. I think 35-37x earnings in 2025 and 2026 is a safe multiple, considering Nvidia topped out between 42-89x in each of the last 4 years. A reversion to 35x on FY25 EPS of $5.49 implies a price target of $153 or +36% from today. See below for my price targets.

To further support my strong buy thesis with more than multiple reversion, here is a simple Discounted Cash Flow analysis using conservative inputs:

  • EPS starts at $4.23 in FY25

  • Grows to $7.13 by FY30, implying ~10.3% annualized growth

  • Terminal multiple at 33x (well below the 5-year average of 58x)

  • Discount rate: 10% (based on a 4.3% risk-free rate + 5.5% equity risk premium)

This yields a fair value of $146/share, or ~30% upside from today's price of $112. Even without assuming upside surprises from Blackwell or broader AI adoption, this DCF demonstrates NVIDIA remains undervalued on fundamentals alone. Let's see how my price target stacks up against other sources.

Guru Focus is quantitative based and shows a fair value of $177 today for Nvidia, perfectly in between my calls for 2025 & 2026. Their chart nicely shows Nvidia routinely trades at or above their "GF Value" with plenty of disconnects. Today, marks one of the larger disconnects from true value.

Tip Ranks polls Wall Street analysts and provides their average price target at $175, in line with what I'd expect one year from now, as well as the GF Value. Notably, the range of estimates is quite wide between the $120 bearish target and $220 for the most bullish. As we'll see in my Best & Worst Buy chart, I think that range is completely reasonable to expect given Nvidia's historical valuation peaks and valleys. While I make my investments primarily anticipating the equity reaching my price target, it's helpful to have an idea of the floor and ceiling. Buy closer to the floor, sell closer to the ceiling, and you'll end up ahead more times than not.

Identifying The Best and Worst Entry Points for Nvidia

My P/E table is an objective historical method to estimate future trading ranges. The Best Buy row outlines the price and multiple paid by those buying at the absolute bottom of the given year based on its earning. The Worst Buy row shows the multiple paid by those who bought at the top. 2022 is certainly an outlier year, and given the large range in values, I'm using median levels to determine a reasonable range for 2025. This implies Nvidia may trade as low as 23x or as high as 70x earnings this year and based on the EPS estimate, $4.37, that gives a $99-303 trading range.

For as fundamentally sound as Nvidia is, it is subject to extreme volatility, especially amid the current trade war. I expect long-term appreciation; however, this is a company that will likely face huge price swings in the short term and may move drastically day after day. I think the trading range I provided gives a good idea of where the general floor and ceiling for the stock is, but looking at the specific highs and low P/Es for each year quickly shows this range can be exceeded in extreme scenarios.

Today, Nvidia trades at 22.1x FY25 earnings, well within "Best Buy" territory, and cheaper than anyone could have purchased in all of 2021. I establish positions at and below the best-buy median, allowing me some comfort knowing I'm not buying at the top. If Nvidia gets as high as 2023's peak, representing the lowest high in the last 4 years, it will fetch $184 or +91% above today's quote within the calendar year. A run up to 2024's high yields a $223 price or +132% from today. See for yourself below.

Transforming a 16% Share Gain Into a 98% Return

Nvidia offers sizable premiums and a plethora of expiration dates to choose from. I'll keep it simple and choose June '26, the closest date that allows long-term capital gains on this attractive opportunity. As Nvidia is a little higher than the floor I set and well below the ceiling, this is the perfect setup for a covered straddle. The covered straddle involves buying 100 shares and simultaneously selling a covered call and put at the same strike and expiration. This effectively lowers the initial capital outlay while providing a significant margin of safety.

I love the covered straddle because it allows me to immediately lower my cost basis and secure a high risk-adjusted return. Nvidia is currently trading at $107.57 at the time of writing. I'm setting up a $125 strike covered straddle position, +16% today's quote. 100 shares would run me $10,757 outright, but lowers to $5,767 after collecting my premiums. See below for the full trade mechanics:

With this type of strategy, only two scenarios are in play. Nvidia trades at or above the $125 strike, or below it in June 2026. The best-case scenario shows an easy 98% annualized gain from no price movement a year from now.

Best-Case Scenario

  • If stock closes at $125.00 or higher, an increase of 16%
  • The calls will be exercised
  • You'll sell your original 100 shares for $12,500
  • The Puts will expire worthless
  • Final Position = No Shares & $12,500
  • Cash on Cash Net Profit ($12,500 - $5,767) = $4,926
  • Total Return = 98%
  • Annualized Return over 434 Days = 98%

As I showed in my valuation, Nvidia will likely trade much higher, around $200 next year. This trade allows you to secure most of the appreciation, at a lower cost basis. If you simply held shares, they would need to run up to $175 for the same return. The alternate scenario is quite appealing as well.

Alternate Outcome (If stock closes below the $125 strike)

  • If stock closes below the $125.00 strike
  • The calls expire worthless
  • You keep the original 100 shares
  • The Puts will be exercised
  • You'll have to buy another 100 shares
  • You will need an additional $12,500 of cash
  • Final Position = 200 shares
  • Start-to-Finish Net Cash Outlay = $-18,267
  • Average Cost = $91.34 ($18,267 / 200)
  • Share price may fall by 15% to breakeven

If Nvidia trades below even the most pessimistic Wall Street price target a year from now, you'll be in positive territory, as your net cost/share will be $91. If I wasn't pursuing this strategy and Nvidia dropped to $91, I'd likely double my position at that valuation. In this scenario, your breakeven is a massive 15% cushion from today's quote. Any price between $92 and $125 will allow for profitable liquidation. At $91 and my $4.37 EPS estimate, Nvidia would trade at about 20.8x earnings, well below the 23x median floor history suggests. This is the perfect scenario to maximize return potential while structuring for a worst-case scenario that will more than likely provide enough cushion for market corrections.

Tariffs, Tension and Competition

Nvidia's long-term thesis is strong, but as with any company, not immune to risks. As the risks are dominating headlines, Nvidia shares have come down considerably, creating what I see as an opportune buying moment.

Geopolitical tensions are at extreme highs right now. Just this week, the U.S. enacted 125% tariffs on Chinese goods, and in response, China has ramped up their tariffs to 84% as well as restricted exports of critical rare earth elements used in chip production. The US has export restrictions of their own, including Nvidia's H20 product, which was restricted for national security. I'd note a positive here is Nvidia's fabless model which is capital light, however with industry-wide hiccups, Nvidia will certainly encounter volatility.

Nvidia's fabless model largely leans on TSMC to fabricate nearly all GPUs, including the H100 Hopper & B100 Blackwell. Heavy reliance on a single supplier caught between US and China tensions rightfully draws scrutiny. That said, Nvidia is quite active in mitigating these risks. The three most notable include:

  • TSMC Arizona Fab - Nvidia will begin sourcing chips directly from TSMC's US-based fab once operational
  • Samsung Collaboration - Nvidia is exploring sourcing less complex chips from other companies like Samsung
  • US AI Systems Assembly - Nvidia announced its Blackwell-based systems will be assembled domestically in the US and that they're looking into building "AI factories" in the future

As for the competitive landscape, AMD is gaining traction with its MI300 series, and Intel announced a joint venture with TSMC to operate US-based chip manufacturing facilities. Both of these moves are targeting Nvidia's dominating market position on AI infrastructure, however, Nvidia maintains its moat by owning the full AI stack. Their focus on the developer ecosystem keeps it squarely a step ahead.

In my opinion, this is why focusing on valuation is so important. Nvidia has a proven business model that generates significant cash flow and earnings on extremely high margins. They're positioned well against macroeconomic problems, and will likely weather this storm better than competitors and most companies. I do expect volatility though, which is why I am pursuing the covered straddle to reduce downside risk.

Conclusion

Nvidia is clearly not just another semiconductor company, it is the AI economy's tool provider powering the next industrial revolution. Its dominance in hardware, software, and data center infrastructure gives it an unprecedented moat, similar to Apple's iOS in mobile computing.

Valuation matters, and while Nvidia isn't trading at its absolute cheapest, the company's structural advantages justify a premium multiple. Using conservative estimates, my 2025 price target of $153 +42% remains in line with fundamental projections and Wall Street expectations.

For investors looking to maximize returns while managing downside risk, structured trades like the covered straddle allow for a 98% return even if Nvidia increases only 16%. Meanwhile, the worst-case scenario results in an effective cost basis of $91, a price below the median historical valuation floor.

Nvidia is a name I've put my money behind, and next to Apple, among the top two best investments in my investing career. Quick doubles and triples in price from here may not come, but I do expect Nvidia to continue outpacing the market in returns. The road won't be linear; however, history suggests a rewarding path for those who stay ahead of the curve. Turn off mainstream media as well as your feelings based on price movement and see valuation as the guiding light.

data.title: Revisiting Greenlight Capital Re: Old Same 'Hold'
data.description:
Greenlight Re (NASDAQ:GLRE) is the reinsurance vehicle used by David Einhorn to invest in the stock markets. The Bermuda-based reinsurer has invested in David Einhorn's funds over the years, supporting the investment decisions of the billionaire. I started to analyze Greenlight Re in 2019 when the stock was valued at around $8.5 per share; at that time, the company was valued at 0.6 times its book value, as investors considered that the reinsurance carrier had a weak underwriting performance.

Since then, the reinsurer has benefited from better pricing conditions, which have impacted the reinsurance industry. Although Greenlight Re succeeded in generating underwriting income in 2022 and 2023, 2024 underwriting performance was loss-making. The reinsurance portfolio generated a pre-tax underwriting loss of circa $8.2 million, suffering from multiple causes: higher number of catastrophe events, like Hurricanes Milton and Helene or the collapse of the Baltimore Bridge coupled with higher attritional loss trends, impacting the current year loss ratio.

In addition, the prior year's claim development was adverse, representing a negative impact of 3.4 points on the loss and the combined ratios, vs. 1.9 points in 2023. Although the reinsurance industry is cyclical and could be impacted by one-off effects, like a higher-than-expected number of catastrophe events, larger peers were more resilient.

Swiss Re (OTCPK:SSREF), Scor (OTCPK:SZCRF), and Munich Re (OTCPK:MURGY) recorded a combined ratio of respectively 89.9%, 86.3%, and 82.2%. Smaller players like SiriusPoint (SPNT) delivered better results, with an 88.0% combined ratio for its reinsurance division.

As I mentioned before in prior articles (I have the impression to repeat myself again and again, maybe I am becoming senile), Greenlight Re is a weak reinsurance carrier, which has only the crumbs of the reinsurance industry, writing some risks as an inadequate price. In 2024, the reinsurance industry recorded an 86.8% combined ratio.

Adjusted from the discounting effect (due to the official introduction of the IFRS17 standards in 2023), the combined ratio was below 92% on average.

Although the industry performance is dominated by reinsurance behemoths like the Germany or Switzerland-based reinsurers, Greenlight Re remains an underperformer. Therefore, investors should not invest in Greenlight by expecting an overcycle overperformance of the reinsurance portfolio. Only the investment performance matters.

Solid Investment Results Overshadowed by Los Angeles Wildfires

As discussed during the Q4 2024 conference call, the Los Angeles wildfires are expected to cost around $50 billion to the insurance industry. Based on the latest estimates, Greenlight's share of this loss will be $15 million to $30 million:

It is early stages, but we estimate the insurance industry loss at $40 billion to $50 billion and anticipate Greenlight's share of this loss will be $15 million to $30 million as we are somewhat underweight in property compared to the industry.

In 2023, an exceptional year in terms of profitability for Greenlight, the reinsurance carrier recorded an underwriting income of circa $32 million, with a combined ratio of 94.5%.

Even if the reinsurer was able to duplicate the same performance in 2023, between half and all the underwriting earnings will be wiped out by the Los Angeles wildfires' costs. Even though the reinsurer succeeds in releasing prior years' reserves, resulting in a positive run-off, the underwriting performance is already endangered for 2025.

In the meantime, the company has already performed well for the first three months of 2025. The investment return was 7.2% on a year-to-date basis.

With the turbulences already observed in April, the unorthodox asset allocation of David Einhorn's fund may bear fruits and generate greater returns for 2025, although it is too soon to make predictions.

In 2024, the reinsurance carrier recorded a solid 9.8% total return, or an investment income of $79.6 million. The contribution of Q1 2024 was significant as the investment performance amounted to 5.2% for the first three months of the year.

In Q1, 2024, the investment portfolio generated an income of $26.4 million.

With both higher return and asset base, Greenlight will undoubtedly generate higher total investment income, which may oscillate between $30 and $40 million. Depending on the magnitude of the wildfire loss costs, most of the investment gains could be used to offset the costs of the Los Angeles wildfire events.

Old Same "HOLD"

Since my first analysis in 2019, I have not changed my mind regarding Greenlight Re and its valuation. Going forward, the reduction in the book value discount remains related to the investment performance of Solasglas, which represents around two-thirds of the overall investment return. With a book value per share of $17.39, the stock is valued at circa 0.75 its book value. It may appear cheap for a reinsurer, and it is.

But there is a good reason for that; investing in Greenlight Re implies that you believe that David Einhorn will invest successfully in Greenlight Re's earned premiums to generate positive returns. Since 2019, the investment portfolio has returned well, although the overall performance was lower than a plain-vanilla S&P500 index. Therefore, I do believe that the book value discount remains as long as David Einhorn does not overperform the traditional stock indexes.

data.title: OPP: Reliant On Lower Interest Rates To Fuel Growth
data.description:
Overview

RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund (OPP) operates as a closed end fund that aims to generate attractive total returns through its portfolio of blended debt investments. Closed end funds and Business Development Companies have been one of my favorite places to collect a high yield in recent years. OPP is unique because it blends these two types of asset classes together in an effort to provide consistent income month over month.

Looking at the performance over the last five years, OPP's price has declined by about 37.2%. However, the total return jumps up above 29% over the same time frame when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders. OPP has a generous starting dividend yield of 13.6%, making it an enticing choice for income seeking investors. As a bonus, distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which may be great for retired investors that depend on supplemental income. Unfortunately, the history of price declines does make me a bit cautious of the fund's stability over a long time frame.

Since OPP is essentially a portfolio of debt investments, I believe that the price has continued to be weighed down by the higher interest rate environment. While the outlook around interest rate cuts aren't favorable, OPP does currently trade at an attractive valuation. OPP has a public inception dating back to 2016, which means that we have nearly a decade worth of performance data to reference. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that OPP implements to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, OPP has total managed assets of $341.6M that is spread across three different strategies. Each different strategy focuses on a different type of asset class, making the portfolio a blended approach of debt investments. The three strategies involve the following:

  1. Opportunistic Income - DoubleLine: 69% of the portfolio.
  2. Alternative Credit Strategy - RiverNorth: 19% of the portfolio.
  3. Tactical CEF Income - RiverNorth: 13% of the portfolio.

The Opportunistic Income portion of the portfolio focuses on agency and non-agency residential and commercial MBS (mortgage backed securities). The Alternative Credit Strategy focuses on a portfolio of small and mid-sized businesses loans that are positioned to be profitable for the fund. Lastly, the Tactical CEF Income portion of the portfolio invests in Business Development Companies, SPACs (special purpose acquisition companies), and traditional ETFs to generate earnings.

Despite the diversified approach, each segment of the fund shares some of the same vulnerabilities. Since the fund relies on debt investments, the overall interest rate environment and borrower defaults will both be influential to OPP's success. To mitigate these risks, OPP remains highly diversified across sectors. Non-Agency MBS investments account for 31% of the fund. This is followed by weight to Agency MBS and small business whole loans, accounting for 20% and 19% of assets respectively.

Lastly, OPP utilizes leverage as part of its strategy in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. The use of leverage currently amounts to 36.4% of assets, which I consider to be quite aggressive in this high interest rate environment. A higher interest rate means that leverage is more costly to use, so management must ensure that it yields positive results. Investors must consider the chance that leverage can be used on a bad investment, and it ends up amplifying losses instead.

Valuation

Since OPP operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of underlying assets. As a result of the recent price decline, OPP now trades at a discount to NAV of 7.3%. For reference, OPP traded at an average discount to NAV of about 9.34% over the last three-year period, which may indicate that OPP currently trades around its fair value. The chart below reveals that OPP has consistently traded at a discount around the 5% mark since its inception, and only rose into the premium territory when interest rates were at near zero levels.

I was curious about whether or not the fund actually generated positive returns on a consistent basis, so I reviewed the most updated semi-annual report dated from December 2024. Net investment income landed at $0.54 per share, while net realized losses were $0.05 per share for 2024. Looking back over prior years, we can see that the majority of OPP's returns have consistently come from net investment income.

This makes sense since the underlying investments rely on interest income from borrowers. The issue seems to be the inconsistent net realizes losses that weigh performance down. For instance, net realized gains were only positive for two out of the last five reporting years. As a result, the NAV seems to be slowly trending downward year over year. Here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $14.91
  • 2021: $15.17
  • 2022: $11.27
  • 2023: $9.61
  • 2024: $9.40
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $9.13

While I don't necessarily think that the NAV declines mean that OPP is a poor fund for investors, I just believe that macro conditions need to be more positively aligned for OPP to thrive. The most important metric seems to be interest rates. If you'll notice from the year-end NAV amounts, the only time the NAV increased was during 2021. This is coincidentally when interest rates were extremely low and SPACs were a lot more popular at the time.

Risk Profile

In order to generate the large yield that OPP does, the portfolio naturally consists of sizable exposure to borrowers that have below investment grade credit ratings. For instance, only 45% of OPP's portfolio contains investments that are rated as invested grade, while another 31% is below investment grade, and 24% is high yield. For simplicity sake, let's assume that all of the high yield portion of the fund is also rated as below investment grade.

The rate of borrower default tends to increase during periods of elevated interest rates. Since the cost of borrowing rises for any borrowers that have debt on a floating rate basis, it can negatively impact operating margins for these businesses. S&P Global estimates that the rate of defaults could increase by 2.5% by September 2025 if rate cuts are delayed.

For reference, the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates throughout 2022 and 2023 in response to record high inflationary data. There have been a few small cuts since then, but we still remain in one of the higher interest rate environments seen over the last decade.

Looking back to OPP's price relationship with the federal funds rate, we can see how OPP's price increased over the span of 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic. A low cost of debt meant that there were a lot more companies seeking capital, which was a great growth catalyst for OPP. The price started to decline as soon as rates were increased.

Therefore, the largest risk to OPP would be continued suppression during a higher rate environment. The Fed continues to leave rates unchanged for now and only estimates there to be one interest rate cut for 2025 due to higher than anticipated inflation. Depending on which direction inflation goes, I think there's even the risk of rates getting increased again at some point. There are so many uncertainties around inflation, the effects of Trump's tariff proposals, and growing unemployment numbers, so I will continue to monitor these external factors.

Dividend

The latest declared monthly distribution amounts to $0.095 per share, bringing the current dividend yield up to 13.6%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.14 per share. The issue is that OPP hasn't had a good track record at being able to generate earnings in excess of this amount on a consistent basis. Many of OPP's years saw a total loss in earnings. For instance, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($0.77) per share.
  • 2021: $2.65 per share.
  • 2022: ($1.62) per share.
  • 2023: $0.19 per share.
  • 2024: $1.24 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.49 per share.

As you can see, there were only two years over the last five, which saw earnings exceed this payout amount. This could be why OPP has continually decreased the dividend payouts since 2019. I unfortunately don't see this changing until the interest rate environment becomes more favorable for the fund. So investors should be aware that distributions may continue to get reduced over the next twelve months.

Referring back to the semi-annual report, large portions of the distribution have also been funded by return of capital in the past. The usage of return of capital could reinforce that the fund hasn't been able to generate a sufficient amount of earnings. Return of capital can also exacerbate the issue of a declining NAV if heavily relied upon. Although, these types of distributions aren't actually taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale.

All of these flaws aside, the high dividend yield has allowed OPP to outperform traditional index-tracking ETFs during the current market uncertainty. On a YTD basis, OPP has outperformed SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). So if there's a market slowdown because of tariffs, the income provided by OPP could help your portfolio remain a bit more resilient.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate OPP as a hold for now, since conditions aren't aligned for success. OPP's portfolio is dependent on ideal interest rate conditions to fuel growth going forward. Interest rate cuts seem unlikely at this time, but long-term investors may benefit from accumulation here while shares trade at a discount to NAV valuation. This reliance on lower interest rates has led to challenges generating a sufficient amount of earnings to support distributions over the last decade. Despite these challenges, the higher distribution received from OPP could be a solid way to offset current market uncertainty.

data.title: Apple Gains From Trump Tariff Reversal, But Is Still Too Pricey
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) stock got a major dose of good luck Friday, as the U.S. government announced that computers and smartphones had been exempted from Donald Trump's China tariffs. Prior to this, the stock was undergoing a severe selloff, as the China tariffs were perceived as having a severe impact on it. Indeed, with 90% of iPhone manufacturing taking place in China, the tariffs probably would have had an impact, had they not been reversed.

It appears likely that Apple stock will experience a rally on Monday because of this news. The stock had actually been holding its own relatively well in the earlier months of Trump’s administration; it was when Trump’s tariffs on China started rising dramatically that it really took a beating.

That’s where we stand today. Down 24% for the year when the NASDQ-100 is down 13%, AAPL is underperforming the index that it is a part of. That is an unusual occurrence. Apple has long been one of the most stable and dependable NASDAQ stocks, owing to its high margins, strong brand identity, and high (historical) growth. However, the company has largely ceased growing, with revenue up 2.6% and earnings down 2% over the trailing 12-month period.

So the situation for Apple is not as fortuitous as it was a few years ago, when its stock was rising rapidly in the market.

If you look at the Apple rating next to this article immediately after reading the text up to this point, you might find the rating confusing. I’ve said that AAPL is likely to rally on Monday; based on this, shouldn’t I think the stock a buy? There are several reasons why I don’t think the likely Monday rally is material to investors:

First, it isn’t feasible for most investors to buy stocks over the weekend through normal channels. AAPL stock will likely open higher in Sunday futures and on the NASDAQ on Monday more or less instantly, meaning only high frequency traders will be able to take advantage of the bounce at open.

Second, a person buying the stock after a bounce occurs, may be paying too high of a price. As I wrote above, Apple had almost no revenue growth and negative earnings growth in the most recent 12-month (TTM) period. Despite this, AAPL trades at 28 times earnings, 7.6 times sales and 44.6 times book value. That is a steep valuation for a company that is not growing. This does not contradict my prediction that AAPL stock will rally on Monday, as investors clearly see it as being worth 28 times earnings--I just disagree with their assessment. Going by the logic that Apple was worth $198 at Friday’s close, it should be worth $198 + X when the NASDAQ opens on Monday, with tariff relief part of the fact set.

When I last covered Apple, I rated the stock a hold on the grounds that it was trading at a nosebleed valuation while having no growth catalysts. I maintain the same rating today; however, since the stock's price now is lower than it was when I last wrote about it, I should explain why I still think it’s a hold at this valuation. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will do that.

Trump’s Tariff Reversal

Before going any further, I should explain what Trump’s about-face on tariffs will likely do for Apple, what it won’t do, and why the stock is likely to have a brief reaction to the move on Monday.

Trump’s tariff reversal will prevent a scenario in which Apple’s hardware becomes 145% more expensive than it was before the tariffs were implemented. That is undoubtedly good news for the company—I mean, good news compared to the situation on Friday, when it looked like the tariffs would be paid.

An entry-level iPhone 16 is listed at $799 on Apple’s website. Unless Apple were to eat some of the cost, a 145% tariff would raise that price to $1,958. Given that customers had been used to $799 entry-level iPhones prior to April 2, a price hike to $1,958 would likely have either depressed revenue (if Apple ate none of the cost), or increased costs. Either way, EBIT and net income would have taken a hit.

Donald Trump’s Friday tariff reversal removes that scenario from the list of apparent outcomes. Those who thought Apple worth $198 on Friday, will likely think it worth more on Monday. So I expect investors to buy Apple stock on Monday. I’m not, however, convinced that the gains will last, for reasons I’ll outline below.

Minimal Growth Prospects

I wrote earlier that Apple’s trailing 12-month growth was lackluster, despite the company’s stock trading at a valuation that needs growth to be sustained. The company’s future growth prospects are also limited, for several reasons.

First, there is the simple fact that Apple is so massive in scale. The company does nearly $400 billion in annual revenue, and 24% of that is pure profit. Finding profitable opportunities at that kind of scale isn’t easy. Simply sinking more money into advertising or producing more of Apple’s existing offerings would likely push the company to the point of diminishing marginal returns. Breaking into new markets, on the other hand, would be difficult, as there are few markets that move the needle for a $400 billion per year business.

We can see the difficulty of finding “needle-moving” businesses for Apple by looking at its recent efforts at doing so.

The first of those was the Vision Pro. This was Apple’s big leap into virtual reality (VR). The product took several years and billions of dollars to develop. Sales ultimately disappointed. Supply chain data indicates that Apple is no longer actively manufacturing the product, although it remains listed on the company's website.

Another was the Apple Car. This never saw the light of day, although Apple spent about a decade and billions of dollars working on it. The project was cancelled in February 2024.

The fact that neither of the above projects got particularly far shows the difficulty of finding meaningful investment opportunities at Apple’s scale.

So, Apple’s avenues to future growth are limited. Investing more money into its current operations would probably lead to diminishing returns, while successful new opportunities are proving hard to find.

Valuation

With the problem with Apple’s lack of growth opportunities is the fact that the company’s stock has a steep valuation. First, let’s look at the multiples that Seeking Alpha Quant has on file.

Apple trades at 28.4 times earnings, 7.6. times sales, 44.6 times book and 27.5 times cash flow. These are growth stock multiples, yet Apple has limited growth prospects. This suggests that the company won't pay investors back--in dividends, buybacks or anything else--in a reasonable timeframe. It’s also worth noting that most of Apple’s multiples are higher than those of its sector, and the IT sector is generally a pricey one.

Next, we can look at Apple’s discounted cash flows. Because of Apple’s limited growth prospects, we can evaluate these with no assumed growth.

In the trailing 12-month period, Apple had $6.45 in FCF per share. Discounted at 4.5% (the current 10-year treasury yield), that’s worth $143. Discounted at 10%, it’s worth $64.50. These figures are both well below Apple’s current price. So, I would not buy Apple stock today.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Apple is that it has a near term catalyst, but is still too pricey. It’s quite likely that Friday’s tariff relief will lead to a bounce on Monday, but the bounce will likely be short-lived. Apple has a great brand and a wide moat, but limited growth opportunities. The most I’d pay for it is about $150.

data.title: Alphabet: The Cheapest Of The Mag 7 And The Least Exposed To Tariffs
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG, GOOGL) has traditionally been one of the most reasonably priced stocks among the Mag 7. Following the broader market selloff that tanked pretty much every US-based equity in the tech sector, Alphabet now looks more compelling than ever.

In fact, among the Mag 7, Google is the least affected by the steep 145% tariff on Chinese goods. That’s mainly because most of its business is based on services, not physical products.

How about Google's Pixel smartphone? - some may ask.

Well, it turns out that the US has introduced (yet another) exemption on tariffs, including smartphones and other electronics components supplied largely by China.

This is great news for the market. In fact, I foresee a V shape recovery over the next 90 days.

As a side note, my portfolio is fully invested at the moment of writing this article, although I don't own Alphabet shares. I own Nvidia and Broadcom (after panic buying them on April 9), although I am considering a full-send move (what's diversification, anyways?) next week in the SP5Y, a 5x leveraged ETF on the S&P 500.

Back to my thesis on Google, there are risks to consider, including the volatile trade policy of the new administration and its implication on inflation and hyperscaler CapEx.

Google - A Safe Haven Amidst Tariffs

Let’s start with a quick look at the history, then, we’ll move on to the numbers.

Google entered the Chinese market in 2006 with a censored version of its search engine (google.cn), reaching 36% market share in just three years. However, due to several cyberattacks and Beijing's stringent censorship rules, Google redirected Chinese users to its Hong Kong site instead. Ultimately, this decision led China's Great Firewall to ban Google's search engine in 2010.

In 2018, Google attempted to reenter the Chinese market through Project Dragonfly, a censored search engine compliant with China's stringent internet regulations. This project faced an internal pushback when Google employees signed a letter raising concerns about its ethics. By July 2019, Google officially ended Project Drangonfly.

Therefore, both Google Services and Google Cloud are safe from tariffs. Not just because these are not available in China but because these business segments are categorized as services and not goods. So far, the tariff drama between the US and China is targeting physical goods only. That said, if tensions keep rising between the US and China, I wouldn’t be surprised if services end up being targeted too.

How about Google's Pixel? Aren't they manufactured in China?

Yes and no. Let me explain.

Back in 2016, Google chose China as its primary manufacturing hub for its new Pixel smartphone. However, in 2019, amidst escalating trade tensions between the US and China during Trump's first administration, Google began relocating a portion of its Pixel smartphone manufacturing to Vietnam. More recently, in 2024, Google further diversified its manufacturing footprint by commencing the assembly of Pixel devices in India. In fact, Dixon Technologies (contract manufacturer of Pixel's and some of Apple's mobile devices) more than doubled its revenue last year, which is a clear sign of how US based companies are diversifying their contract manufacturers away from China.

Unfortunately, Google does not break down Pixel sales in its financial reporting. All we have is a mention in the 10-K, stating that "devices, which primarily include sales of the Pixel family" are categorized within the Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices subsegment within Google Services.

Despite not knowing the percentage of Pixel units manufactured in China and imported into the US, I argue that this figure is irrelevant. Why?

This Friday afternoon, approximately one hour before the end of the trading session, the US announced that a list of tech products, including smartphones, laptop computers, and chip making equipment, will be exempt from the reciprocal tariffs. So, the 145% tariff on imports from China doesn't apply to Google's Pixel phones (or any smartphones).

This, combined with the fact that the tariffs only apply to goods and not services, keeps Google’s earnings largely untouched. At least during the next 90 days.

ChatGPT Isn’t Replacing Google Search Anytime Soon

When the AI boom took off in mid-2023, one of my biggest worries was that Google Search might soon become useless. However, Alphabet's FY 2024 results are clear proof that search is still going strong and continues to be a key part of their business.

Take a look at the table below, particularly in the % change in revenue for google and youtube ads.

Do you see the double digit revenue increase in search and YouTube ads? Well, that’s clear proof for skeptics (like me, not too long ago) that Google Search isn’t becoming obsolete because of AI. Just look at the top keywords by PPC, and you will quickly realize that people don't use AI (at the moment) to search for these services. When you're looking for a business or a product, Google Search is the go-to platform. I don't see this changing anytime soon. In fact, Google is now using Gemini for AI overviews on search queries, reaching more than 1 billion users across 100 countries, according to the latest 10-K.

Furthermore, Google's Gemini is advancing towards its goal of reaching 500 million users by the end of 2025. That said, even if they reach this target, they would still be lagging behind ChatGPT, which has approximately 400 million weekly users. So far, Alphabet's latest LLM developments include Gemini 2.5 Flash, a streamlined version of the Gemini 2.5 Pro that allows developers to adjust processing time based on query complexity. On top of that, Deep Research has now become available to Gemini Advanced subscribers utilizing the Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental model.

Valuation

I am about to prove that Alphabet is the cheapest and least exposed company to tariffs among the Mag 7.

I considered building the table below, showing a valuation comparison by P/E, P/S, P/CF, and P/FCF (all TTM values, as I don't trust the Street's estimates for next year). Additionally, I considered adding a column showing CapEx.

On a P/E basis, Alphabet is trading at just 18x last year's earnings. That's the cheapest ratio among the entire Mag 7 group. On a P/S basis, Alphabet is the second cheapest after Amazon. This makes sense, considering Amazon's massive online retail business. As for me, I prefer earnings over just sales.

On a price to cash flow basis (both OCF and FCF), Alphabet is nearly tied with META. Both companies look quite attractive on this metric. If I had to pick between Meta and Alphabet, I’d go with Alphabet since it looks more attractive on a P/S and P/E basis.

Naturally, the broader market selloff has brought Alphabet's valuation (P/E, P/CF) significantly below its 5-year median.

Overall, I see a company undervalued among its peers and its own historical performance.

Risks

Despite the tariff drama that we have experienced over the past 6-8 weeks, Google has reaffirmed its $75 billion CapEx spending plan for this year. Additionally, as I said before, the tariffs are not applicable to chip making equipment. Therefore, I don't forsee an increase in GPU costs, at least, not within the next 90 days. Given how unpredictable US trade policy has been over the past weeks, no one knows what will happen after the 90 day pause. This is a risk to consider. In fact, this is the exact reason why my bull thesis has a timeframe of 90 days.

The biggest risk I see now is (yet) another plot twist, where the new administration changes its mind again and goes ahead with semiconductor tariffs. If that's the case, I believe we could see a period of high inflation in the US, close to 2022 levels (only that this time, inflation would be driven by one man, not by an overly stimulated economy).

If semiconductor tariffs kick in, I think Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Google will scale back their CapEx spending this year. That could trigger a selloff in the tech sector since the skyhigh valuations (just look at Palantir's 400 P/E ratio) have been supported by the AI narrative. If enterprises and consumers don’t adopt AI as quickly as the market expects, there could be more trouble ahead.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I see Alphabet as one of the most undervalued stocks among the Mag 7, with the least exposure to tariffs due to the nature of its business. Current trade policies with China don't affect services like Google Search and Cloud, as Google is not operating in this region. Additionally, Pixel phones manufactured in China are exempt from the new tariffs, while the company is diversifying manufacturing outside of China.

Furthermore, Google Search is not becoming obsolete due to AI, as noted by the double digit revenue growth in FY 2024. Therefore, I rate Google as a strong buy, with a timeframe of approximately 90 days in mind. Risks include deterioration in the current trade policy due to the unpredictable decisions of the current administration.

data.title: PCMM: Why This Private Credit ETF Deserves A Spot On Your Watchlist
data.description:
For investors feeling spooked about the volatility found in equities, one might find some relative stability in the credit market. We now have more choices than ever for these high-quality ETFs, and this article will explore one that has recently found its way on my radar.

Overview

The BondBloxx Private Credit CLO ETF (NASDAQ:PCMM) offers investors a way to access the private credit collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) market, which was traditionally reserved for institutional and ultra-high-net-worth investors. This fund is actively managed and focuses on CLOs made to mid-sized companies. Specifically, PCMM targets firms with an annual EBITDA between $10 million and $250 million. PCMM is brand new, having launched in December 2024.

The ETF is actively managed and has an expense ratio of 0.68%, along with a yield to maturity of 8.31%. PCMM pays a monthly dividend and has an annual yield of 1.82%. Thus, for those looking for pure income opportunities, PCMM might deserve a pass. However, for those seeking capital preservation as well as diversification from traditional bonds and public credit markets, PCMM adequately fills this niche use case. With PCMM allowing one to access private credit without locking up capital in private funds, it reduces the barrier of entry significantly. It has $107.62 million worth of assets under management.

Portfolio Structure

PCMM's portfolio structure is unique in that at least 80% of its assets must be allocated to private credit CLO tranches. PCMM also attempts to maintain an average credit rating of "A" and avoids equity tranches altogether.

At the time of writing it holds 54 CLO securities, with most of them being senior class A notes that have credit ratings that range from AAA to A, with some minor additional allocations to BBB and BB tranches.

PCMM has 96.6% of its securities in the United States, and it has a notable concentration in its top 10 holdings, with these assets accounting for 52% of its total portfolio. While this may seem concentrated at first glance, CLOs generally comprise hundreds of loans, which gives PCMM a surprisingly resilient portfolio structure.

In addition to having the sheer breadth of loans, PCMM also invests in a range of industries, with Software being the dominant sector.

Name % of Net Assets
Software 11.40%
Health Care Providers & Services 7.90%
Commercial Services & Supplies 5.40%
Professional Services 5.30%
Insurance 5.20%
Diversified Consumer Services 3.40%
Health Care Technology 3.20%
IT Services 2.80%
Health Care Equipment & Supplies 2.30%
Containers & Packaging 1.90%
Other 51.00%

One thing that investors may appreciate about PCMM's portfolio is that the fund's duration is near-zero at 0.06 years, as all of its holdings are pegged to a floating-rate. This means that it is insulated from interest rate risks.

Outlook

My outlook for the private credit market is positive, especially for CLOs. U.S. volumes for CLOs surged, hitting $202 billion, with middle-market CLOs making up $38 billion of that total figure.

My view is that CLOs will continue to perform strongly this year as well as into 2026. The reason is that the consensus is that the Fed is expected to keep interest rates elevated in order to curb inflation. According to the Fed, tariffs are a key reason why it could lead to an inflationary environment.

Furthermore, despite the higher rates, default rates are generally low, especially among middle-market loans that PCMM specializes in. In the direct lending market as a whole, the default rate stands at 1.9%, which is 1% below the long-term average.

Finally, it's also key to remember that middle-market CLOs are relatively new, having only gained serious attention post-2015. The explosive growth of private credit led to this growth, and I believe that institutional investors will continue to pour money into them, as many have attractive risk-adjusted risk profiles, which is ideal for pension funds, insurance companies, and asset managers to hold for the long run.

Peer Analysis

Perhaps due to being released very recently, PCMM is in an unfavorable position on a net yield basis when one compares it against its peers in the table below. For those seeking high income, a mix of JAAA and JBBB or CLOZ could lead to a combined dividend yield in the high 6% range. This could successfully balance the need for high yield as well as credit and market risks.

ETF Issuer Credit Focus Risk Level Expense Ratio Dividend Yield Net Yield Notes

Focused on newer, less liquid private credit CLOs
JAAA Janus Henderson AAA-rated CLO tranches Low 0.21% 6.13% 5.92%

Conservative, high-quality CLO exposure
JBBB Janus Henderson B to BBB-rated CLO tranches Moderate 0.49% 7.85% 7.36%

Targets lower investment grade for more income
BCLO BlackRock (iShares) BBB to B- CLO tranches Moderate 0.45% 1.30% 0.85%

Yield-focused with a big asset manager behind it
CLOZ Panagram BBB to B CLO tranches Moderate–High 0.50% 8.89% 8.39%

Newer ETF, high-yield exposure to riskier CLO segments

PCMM is also unattractive due to having the highest expense ratio, while also offering the lowest dividend yield.

However, it's important to note that since PCMM is brand-new, its trailing dividend yield is misleading, since it's only accounting for three of the monthly dividends it has paid out. When using the 30-day SEC yield, PCMM's performance is actually above average, as one can see in the table below. The caveat is that it's better than what it appears at first glance, but when one takes its higher expense ratio into consideration, it's a little bit on the pricier side without an edge that has materialized yet.

Ticker

30-Day SEC Yield
PCMM 7.29%
JAA 5.45%
JBBB 7.54%
BCLO 7.82%

Finally, these ETFs are not immune to market stresses, as they still have at least some correlation with equities. For instance, the more speculative ETFs like JBBB sold off hard during the market panic of Trump's tariffs, but JAAA's credit quality insulated it from dropping even further.

Risks

A key risk for PCMM is that it invests in private credit CLOs, which are less liquid than those that are traded publicly. With less volume naturally comes wider spreads and reduced flexibility for buying and selling the securities. What this means in practice is that these spreads could lead to an erosion in the fund's NAV during times of high market stress.

The other important risk I see is that it has a very limited operating history, and although its SEC yield is competitive, we have yet to see it materialize in the form of dividends and returns for shareholders. PCMM is competing against some very well-known and trusted ETFs like JAAA or JBBB with longer operating histories, which makes it comparably more risky in this regard.

Conclusion

I rate PCMM as a Hold, and it is an ETF for income investors to keep on their watch lists. I'd like to see the expense ratio lower as it accumulates more assets under management, and for it to prove that it has a clear edge over its peers before making a Buy recommendation. It has all the pieces to potentially offer better risk-adjusted returns through its portfolio, but some confirmation is required to validate this hypothesis.

data.title: NATO: Tariff War Helps Raise Defense Stocks Over The Market
data.description:
As President Donald J. Trump emerged victorious in the 2024 election and prepared to begin his second term, his team informed European officials that he expects their respective countries to spend at least 5% of their Gross Domestic Product (GDP) on building military capabilities.

As of 2024, it was estimated that virtually every country - even the U.S. - fell short of this target.

However, it bears noting that the U.S.' spending is significantly higher than that of almost every one of its European allies combined. Additionally, given the significant deployment of U.S. military assets over in Europe to buttress its defense from threats, it is intuitively logical that Europe could ideally stand to be more invested in its own defense (and an idea supported by many a U.S. think tank as well).

In the wake of President Trump's re-election, plans were underway within the European Union (E.U.) to raise spending by €500 billion per year, which the European Commissioner for Defense Industry and Space Andrius Kubilius argued was only enough to cover an air defense program. Also, many of the bloc's nations balked at the prospect of raising the annual target from 2024's 2% to a mere 3% (which would cost the E.U. around €200 billion), due to economic difficulties and already-stretched balance sheets. Poland, an already-aggressive spender owing to its proximity to the ongoing Russo-Ukrainian conflict, suggested one of two methods to raise the required capital: the E.U. could sell more joint bonds backed by its own long-term budget or create a new special purpose vehicle modelled on the eurozone bailout fund, the European Stability Mechanism, to provide loans by selling bonds backed by paid-in and callable capital from participating countries.

The first option found few takers but the potential for non-E.U. countries such as the United Kingdom and Norway to participate in the second option created some buzz; however, markets expect that this "new borrower" would have pay a premium over the interest the E.U. pays for its bonds - thus adding to the cost burden.

In light of these difficulties, President Trump's demand for spending to go up by an ever larger margin had found few enthusiastic takers. On the 6th of March this year, the European Commission proposed the "ReArm Europe" plan, which aimed to raise €650 billion over four years (i.e. an average of €175 billion each year) by allowing member nations to increase their military budgets to up to 1.5% of GDP without being counted in their national deficits and raising €150 billion through EU-issued bonds, which would then be lent to member states at low-interest rates and long repayment terms.

The plan almost instantly ran into trouble, with Italy and Spain demanding broader definition of the plan to include anti-terrorism, satellite communication, artificial intelligence and quantum computing capabilities. Both nations also expressed discomfort with the name, with Spain stating outright that Southern Europe faces threats that are altogether different from those faced by Eastern Europe. The plan's name was amended to "Readiness 2030".

Steering Away from U.S. Dependence?

E.U. officials proposed that European countries can partake in the €150 billion loan program - titled "SAFE" (Security Action for Europe) - if the products being procured have a definitive Europe connection. For ammunition, missiles and small drones, 65% of the costs must originate inside the E.U., European Economic Area (EEA), European Free Trade Association (EFTA) countries or Ukraine, with the manufacturer being located within these blocs and not controlled by another country. For more complex systems such as air and missile defense systems and larger drones, it must be possible to substitute components that could be subject to restrictions imposed by other countries.

While it is possible for an E.U.-based subsidiary of a British or U.S. company, the cost origination aspect implies that weapons manufactured in a British or American plant might not be eligible - a move that rankled U.S. officials, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio reportedly warning Europe that any exclusion of U.S. companies would be seen negatively by Washington. However, in light of the ongoing tariff war, some degree of market access limitation might be imposed on U.S. manufacturers.

This warning has a certain logic: outside of Ukraine (which became the world’s largest importer of major arms in the 2020–24 period), arms imports by the E.U. more than doubled between 2015–19 and 2020–24, with the U.S. supplying 64% of these arms.

With arms supplied to 107 countries in 2020-24, U.S. arm exports increased by 21% between 2015–19 and 2020–24, with its share of global arms exports growing from 35% to 43%. In effect, the U.S. is resolutely the world's largest arms exporter - with France a very distant second.

For the first time in two decades, the largest share of U.S. arms exports in 2020–24 went to Europe (35%) rather than the Middle East (33%), thus possibly underlining Secretary Rubio's concerns.

Despite calls from within Europe to limit dependence on U.S. imports, European NATO members have over 500 combat aircraft and many other complex weapons systems still on order from U.S. suppliers. However, with "smaller" weapons and systems, it is likely that European manufacturers might be net beneficiaries.

Market Impact

While it is by now common knowledge that the broad market S&P 500 (SPX in index form; SPY in ETF form) has been under significant pressure and volatility in the lead-up to President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, defense stocks have shown a widely varying trajectory as driven by investor convictions:

Despite both being European companies that are potentially beneficiaries of the E.U.'s largesse for arms buying, German arms manufacturer Rheinmetall AG (OTCPK:RNMBF; OTCPK:RNMBY) substantially outperforms relative to France's engine manufacturer Safran S.A. (OTCPK:SAFRF; OTCPK:SAFRY). Despite being in the same industry as Safran (i.e. aviation systems) while potentially impacted by E.U. limitations, U.S. aviation technology majors Northrop Grumman (NOC) and Lockheed Martin (LMT) have been somewhat resurgent.

Rheinmetall is expected to be a wide-ranging beneficiary from increases in E.U.'s expenditure on projectile weapon systems and munitions while historical "big-ticket" buy-ins of aviation systems by the likes of Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin by European armies are unlikely to be substituted out before said systems' end of life. Also, given the top-of-the-line performance characteristics by these systems, it's unlikely that European armies would be willing to settle for less or not be receptive to next-generation products. This creates some bottlenecks for the likes of Safran as they're found predominantly in French/European-produced aircraft.

Rheinmetall, Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin also outperform the broad market by a comfortable margin. Meanwhile, the Themes Transatlantic Defense ETF (NASDAQ:NATO) - which includes all four companies along many other manufacturers - pulls at nearly 10% outperformance relative to the S&P 500 as measured from the end of February till the 11th of April. After a period of decline, the "NATO" ETF is now drawing flat relative to the end of February and has a slight bullish momentum being imputed.

A "Balanced" Investing Style in the Sector?

The "NATO", Themes Transatlantic Defense ETF is a more recent entrant in the defense ETFs space, where the likes of the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) and the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) are likely to be better known to investors. Both of the latter solely feature U.S. tickers, with the top 10 holdings being strikingly similar, albeit different in terms of weight distribution.

The "NATO" ETF, on the other hand (and as the name suggests), is a little more international:

While the top 10 holdings for the "NATO" ETF is closer in terms of weight distribution to that of Invesco's "PPA" ETF, it features the solid outperformer Rheinmetall in the top position. Also, the Top 10 holdings have a near-even balance between U.S. and E.U. tickers.

In overall holdings, however, U.S. tickers do make up almost 60% of the ETF by weight. But with the remainder being driven by tickers from the Continent, it has a profile that is distinct from both the U.S. ETFs.

An interesting feature is that the ETF assigns a slightly higher average constituent weight to European (i.e. EU, EEA and EFTA) tickers than it does to tickers from other regions, including the U.S.

In Conclusion

The favorable representation of European tickers in the "NATO" ETF might prove to be an interesting feature that might enable investors to capture the uptrend in European military spending. At the same time, it is unlikely that European nations would commit vast sums of capital to develop alternative "high-ticket" weapon systems that are entirely distinct or free from U.S. suppliers.

Overall, regardless of economic conditions, it is increasingly likely that nations in the Western Hemisphere remain locked into sustained and increased military spending, potentially creating ample opportunities for outperformance versus the market. The "NATO" ETF's distribution of weights across regions adds an interesting layer of consideration for investors looking to capitalize on spending patterns being implied by Western governments. It might make sense for investors to consider diversifying their portfolio by including this relatively recent instrument.
