data.title: The Profit Engine: A Financial Anatomy Of Union Pacific
data.description:
Introduction

Union Pacific (NYSE:UNP) today is the most powerful transportation artery in the western United States: more than 32,000 miles of tracks, 8,300 locomotives, and about 10,000 customers. UP connects the Pacific Coast, Mexico and the industrial heart of America — the Midwest. And, importantly, it is the only company in the country with access to all six key crossings on the border with Mexico — a real advantage in an era when logistics is increasingly becoming a matter of geopolitics.

Recent years have been difficult but important for Union Pacific. The transition to the Exact schedule model (PSR) proved to be painful, especially against the background of the staffing shortage in 2022. With the arrival of Jim Vena in 2023, the strategy was reversed: from cost reduction, the focus has shifted to stability, reliability and growth. Already in 2024, this gave a result — the level of security on the network increased significantly, and the volume of international containers in the second half of the year soared by 30% compared to the same period in 2023. At the same time, the company is upgrading its technological infrastructure. The modernization of all major operating systems, including the introduction of NetControl, has been completed. In 2024, Union Pacific emerged from protracted turbulence with a flexible, technological business.

Business model

Union Pacific is a huge logistics machine built into the economy of the whole country. They earn money by moving goods through their colossal network, which stretches for more than 32 thousand miles.

Their clients range from farmers and energy companies to retailers and chemists, that is, the entire spectrum of those who need to transport large and heavy goods. The cost of the trip depends on the weight, distance, type of cargo, and even the cost of fuel.

They divide the entire business into three categories: Bulk is a bulk like coal, grain and fertilizers; Industrial is everything industrial: chemicals, building materials, oil, metals; and Premium is the top segment where containers and cars are spinning. It is Premium that generates the most margin, especially given the revival of global trade. Their entire network operates end-to-end: routes are centralized from the headquarters in Omaha, cargo is safely transferred between railways and regional lines, and the entire infrastructure from terminals to tracks is used as flexibly as possible.

The year 2024 has just shown how the company is able to adapt. Bulk sank — the winter was mild, gas was cheap, and coal went down. Grain and fertilizers gained a little, but the segment as a whole passed. At Industrial, volumes dropped slightly, but prices went up, and this saved revenue. The transportation of oil and chemicals was particularly impressive. But Premium has become a real driver. After a rough start to the year, the second half showed strong growth — more than 30% year-on-year for intermodal transport, when cargo was reoriented from the East and Canada back to the West. And even with cars, everything was not bad — despite the downtime at the factories, new contracts allowed us to keep volumes.

Union Pacific in general has transformed a lot in recent years. Previously, it lived mainly on coal and grain, but now it relies on marginal segments such as containers, automobiles, and industry. The PSR model has improved productivity: fewer locomotives, fewer crews, but more cargo per train. But at the same time, the company has kept the capacity in reserve so as not to lose flexibility. This helps to cope with peak loads and not to drop the service. Plus, they are actively integrating with autologistics, digital services for customers, big data and green technologies.

Competitors

Rail freight transportation in the USA is a mature market, where six large Class I operators have long divided the entire map among themselves. And in this field, Union Pacific is one of the key players in the West. Together with BNSF, they actually control the entire backbone logistics from the Pacific coast to Chicago. UP interacts more with eastern players like CSX Corporation (CSX) and Norfolk Southern (NSC) through connections, providing transcontinental routes. In 2023, another player appeared on the arena — CPKC (CP), formed after the merger of Canadian Pacific and Kansas City Southern, and it was he who intensified the north-south competition, especially in the Mexican direction. But even so, the main challenge for UP is not other railways at all, but motor transport, which, due to its flexibility, still picks up a significant part of the cargo, especially in the range of 500-1500 miles.

From the point of view of macroeconomics, 2024 passed under the flag of stagnation. The entire industry showed an increase of only 0.3%, and the drivers were not traditional coal or construction goods, but the recovery of the automotive industry and the growth of intermodal transportation. As for the struggle within the railway industry, UP's main competitor is still BNSF. They run parallel on many routes, especially from West Coast ports to Chicago. Yes, BNSF has long had a reputation as an operator with a more flexible service, but over the past two years, Union Pacific has really improved. Their metrics for speed, turnover of wagons, and accuracy of delivery have improved, and customers are starting to notice this. Cooperation with eastern roads is based on the principle of "together against others" — they cooperate at junctions in order to maintain competitiveness against trucks. But with CPKC, the situation is getting worse — it is no longer a partner, but a full-fledged rival in the southern direction.

But the toughest competition is still against trucking. And that's where UP got a good window of opportunity in 2024. High fuel prices, a shortage of drivers, and increased customer ESG campaigns are playing into the hands of the railways. UP actively promotes the topic of ecology — CO₂ emissions are 75% lower compared to trucks. But in order for customers to actually transfer to the rails, ESG alone is not enough — it is necessary that the cargo arrives on time. After severe criticism in 2022, Union Pacific worked on the mistakes: expanded the pool of machinists, introduced a reserve of locomotives, updated processes — and in 2024 the network became noticeably more flexible. These improvements have really helped to take back some of the intermodal traffic, especially in the second half of the year.

The final scenario at the entrance to 2025 is as follows: the market is growing slowly, there are enough challenges, but Union Pacific is holding on confidently. Plus the infrastructure, reach, and customer base — all this provides a powerful foundation to hold positions and aim to increase the share where it really works.

Financials

After the disastrous 2020, when the pandemic hit the entire industry sharply, UP quickly regained its position: in 2021, revenue grew at double-digit rates. In 2024, Union Pacific continued to grow, increasing operating revenue to $24.25 billion— up 1% year-on-year. Volumes went up by 2%, and tariffs went up too, although the drop in diesel prices slowed down the final result a little.

I expect revenue growth in the range of 2-3% in 2025, to $24.8–25.0 billion. This is a moderate but realistic scenario: a small plus in terms of volumes (coal and intermodal), plus a moderate indexation of tariffs. I expect that UP will partially shift inflation to customers and improve the structure of the cargo portfolio. Intermodal will drop slightly in the first half of the year due to the high base, but the trend should level off in the second half of the year. Bulk may increase against the background of stable gas prices, Industrial will be stable, and Premium is the main candidate for growth. On the horizon of 2026-2027, I see an acceleration to 3-4% per year and a potential revenue of over $27 billion. This does not look like an ambitious over—plan; rather, it is a realistic path against the background of soft economic growth and the continued flow of goods to the railway.

In recent years, Union Pacific has been steadily increasing margins, and 2024 proved this: the company generated $12.1 billion in EBITDA with a margin of about 50%, and net profit increased to $6.747 billion, which is almost 28% of net margin. Operating profit increased by 7% to $9.7 billion. All this despite the fact that revenue increased by only 1% — there is a competent cost management.

I conservatively expect that by 2027, the EBITDA margin will remain at the current level in the range of 49-50%, and the absolute EBITDA will reach $13-14 billion. Due to digitalization, automation, and new technology, costs should grow more slowly than inflation. An important point is that even with rising salaries, UP keeps spending growth at 3-4% per year. If fuel prices remain moderate and the company continues to upgrade its fleet of locomotives, the savings will be consolidated in the model. I would also like to note a reduction in interest expenses to $1.269 billion and an effective tax rate of around 24%. All this will allow the company to gradually achieve a net profit above $7.5 billion by 2027 with a margin of about 28%.

The financial position of Union Pacific at the end of 2024 looks stable and confident. The company's total debt has decreased to $31.2 billion, and the Debt/EBITDA indicator remains at a comfortable level of about 2.67x, which is quite acceptable for capital-intensive infrastructure. At the same time, interest payments do not put pressure on business coverage at the level of 7.93x. Importantly, the company does not just service debt, but uses excess free cash flow to reduce it: in 2024, UP repaid debts of $2.2 billion, while raising only $800 million in new debt. Net debt has decreased, and the debt structure remains predominantly fixed, with low sensitivity to rates.

Capital expenditures in 2024 amounted to $3.45 billion, or about 14% of total revenue. This is a typical level for a railway (not wasteful or undervalued). For example, part of the capital went to the early purchase of leased locomotives (advantageous at high rental rates). In the future, I expect Capex to grow slightly at face value, but at about a stable 13-14% of revenue, which coincides with the forecast of management. In other words, the company does not require increased investments to achieve its strategic goals – the network has sufficient capacity after past expansions, and focusing on efficiency means making better use of existing assets.

FCF in 2024 amounted to about $6.65 billion (+24% YoY). In 2025-2027, my model assumes the growth of CFOs to $9.7–10 billion with a stable CapEx of about $3.1-3.4 billion, which allows generating more than $7.5 billion FCF annually. This is enough to cover dividends, smoothly increase cashbacks, and maintain flexibility. At the same time, investments remain rational — the company does not spend excessively, but focuses on improving efficiency and profitability. Even with moderate revenue growth (3-4% per year) I expect the outstripping growth of free cash flow.

In 2024, the dividend per share was $5.28 with a yield of about 2.38%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $3.251 billion in dividends or $5.38 per share with a yield of 2.41%.

Valuation

The company's shares have fallen by 7.17% over the past year, and the main drop occurred in April 2025 after the news about the introduction of tariffs, the entire market fell sharply. At the same time, Union Pacific has lagged behind market growth over the past 12 months, as the S&P 500 has grown by 4.74%. I believe that after the April correction, the company's shares have gained an upside in price by 9-10%, which can be used.

The company is trading at a discount to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the discount on P/E is 17%, and on EV/EBITDA about 25%.

At the same time, compared with peers, the company is trading near average values in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers, and at a premium in terms of P/B and P/S, as can be seen in the table below. It can also be noted that Union Pacific is the most profitable company among peers and does not lag behind the market average in terms of revenue growth.

In my forecast of the target price, I use four estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 20%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 6.87%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 21.95x, P/S is 6.13x, and EV/EBITDA is 14.49x (took historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $245.06 with a growth potential of 9.63% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 12.04%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall Street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the general view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $257.63 with an upside of about 15%.

Key risks

The most obvious risk is the very nature of the market: it is mature, overheated and growing very slowly, which calls into question the possibility of large—scale expansion. Against this background, the emergence of CPKC with fresh infrastructure and ambitions to take over north-south transportation — especially through Mexico — looks like a real challenge. Add to this competition from motor transport and you get a very aggressive environment. Trucks are more flexible, faster on short distances, and customers can easily swap a railroad for a truck if disruptions start somewhere. And failures are not that uncommon: the year 2022 showed that even giants like UP have serious reliability problems. In addition, the macro. A decline in industrial production, a new wave of trade wars, or a possible increase in Federal Reserve interest rates can all instantly hit cargo flows.

Key growth drivers

With the arrival of Jim Vienna, a serious reboot began: the focus is not on reducing for the sake of reporting, but on real growth and improving the quality of service. And the first results have already been achieved, especially in intermodal transport, which is returning to its previous volumes after the recession, especially in connection with the ports of the West Coast. The key bet on environmental friendliness is also starting to work: corporate clients are increasingly looking for “green” logistics, and in terms of emissions, UP looks very convincing. The company's network is generally a separate story. UP controls all major crossings with Mexico, and this gives it a huge advantage in a situation where cross-border trade is growing. Plus, strong financial indicators include an EBITDA margin of about 50% in 2024, high free cash flow, and a manageable debt burden. This gives the company the opportunity not to choose between investments, dividends and buybacks — it can do everything at once.

Conclusion

Union Pacific combines moderate revenue growth with improving margins, leading to faster profit growth. The company generates high free cash flow, which allows it to reduce debts, finance new initiatives, and direct money to cashback or dividends. Return on equity remains high (over 15% ROIC), reflecting the success of investments and barriers to entry in the industry. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $245.06 with a growth potential of 9.63% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 12.04%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Unexpectedly Falls In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Wholesale inflation unexpectedly fell in March, dropping for the first time in 17 months. The producer price index for final demand was down 0.4% month-over-month after a 0.1% increase in February. This was lower than the expected 0.2% growth. On an annual basis, headline PPI increased 2.7%, down from 3.2% in February and below the 3.3% forecast.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy, fell 0.1% in March, down from 0.1% in February and lower than the expected 0.3% growth. This marks the first monthly decline since July. On an annual basis, core PPI eased to 3.3% from 3.5% in February and came in below the 3.6% forecast.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI. Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In March, the PPI for finished goods fell 1.3% month-over-month, down from 0.2% in February. This is the largest monthly decline in 17 months. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods slowed to 1.0% from 2.1% in February. Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.2% on the month, down from the 0.3% in February. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods was unchanged at 2.3%.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective. PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer. With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

For a closer look at the two main measures of inflation (CPI and PCE) and how they stack up against each other, check out here. Note: data is through January 2024.
